svg.text: The network map handed over in plain sight
/etc/passwd + /etc/hosts = full reconnaissance without a single suspicious packet

g.text: Karim is an intern. He has read-only SSH access to the dev server. Nothing more.
By reading /etc/hosts (world-readable by default), Karim maps the internal network:
This file lists every system account. Karim spots active service accounts with shells:
He now knows WHO (active accounts with a shell) and WHERE (IPs of critical servers).
Zero network scan performed. No IDS triggered anything.
Karim tries the "backupuser" credentials against the NAS (192.168.1.20).
Default password never changed. Full access to the backup NAS granted:
3 years of database snapshots, fully accessible and exfiltrated.
Backups are neither encrypted nor audited. The exfiltration goes unnoticed for 6 weeks.

svg.text: An intern with limited SSH access mapped the entire infrastructure
by reading two "harmless" files — without triggering a single alert.
